nativeRange.habitat: Coastal brackish estuaries, tidal rivers, and slow-moving lowland streams; also enters adjacent freshwater reaches; bottom-oriented in shallow, often turbid water with sandy or muddy substrate and emergent vegetation

decorPreferences: rocks and caves creating nooks and crannies
hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias, mangrove root)
driftwood for cover and territory definition
crushed coral or aragonite substrate optional (buffers pH and KH)

diet.notes: Strict carnivore requiring hard-shelled prey to wear down continuously growing fused teeth (beak); without regular hard-shelled food the beak overgrows, preventing feeding and causing death. Primary staples: live or frozen snails (bladder, ramshorn, nerite), mussels, cockles, oyster meat, krill, and ghost shrimp. Supplementary foods: frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae. Does not readily accept dried or pellet foods. Feeding live snails 2–3 times per week is strongly advised for beak maintenance. Avoid overfeeding — puffers will eat until overfull.

goodWith: bumblebee gobies (Brachygobius spp.) in brackish setups
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish (severe fin-nipping risk)
conspecifics without ample space and visual breaks
Green Spotted Puffer (Dichotomyctere nigroviridis — much larger and more aggressive)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Tetraodon biocellatus (synonym). Check current availability on FishBase summary page.

summary: Small (~8 cm) SE Asian puffer named for its yellow figure-8 back markings. Euryhaline — thrives in low-end brackish (SG 1.005–1.010); pure-freshwater specimens typically decline over time. Requires live snails regularly to wear down continuously growing beak. Intermediate care; best kept singly in a species-only brackish setup.
careNotes:
The Figure Eight Puffer is one of the most popular brackish-water aquarium fish, prized for its bold personality, pattern recognition of owners, and entertaining feeding behaviour. Despite being frequently sold and labelled as a freshwater species, it is fundamentally euryhaline — native to coastal tidal estuaries and brackish rivers of Southeast Asia — and the consensus among experienced keepers is that long-term health is significantly better in low-end brackish water (SG 1.005–1.010) than in pure freshwater. Specimens maintained in pure freshwater often survive for a year or two but suffer elevated disease rates and shortened lifespans; at SG 1.005–1.008, lifespans of 10–15 years have been documented.

A minimum tank of 75 L (20 gal US) with a 75 cm footprint suits one specimen. An additional 20–30 L per extra fish is needed if attempting group housing, which requires a spacious, heavily decorated tank with multiple visual breaks. Because this species is an aggressive fin-nipper, only fast-swimming, short-finned brackish tankmates such as bumblebee gobies and salt-tolerant mollies are generally suitable.

Beak maintenance is mandatory: the fused teeth grow continuously and must be worn down through regular consumption of hard-shelled prey. Live ramshorn or bladder snails offered 2–3 times per week are the standard solution. Overgrown beaks require veterinary trimming under anaesthesia. Puffers produce considerable waste; powerful filtration and frequent partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are essential. This species should not be confused with the Green Spotted Puffer (Dichotomyctere nigroviridis), which reaches 15 cm, is far more aggressive, and requires full marine salinity as an adult.

Taxonomic note: reclassified from Tetraodon to Dichotomyctere (Kottelat 2013); older literature uses Tetraodon biocellatus or Tetraodon ocellatus. "Dichotomyctere ocellatus" is the current accepted name.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is extremely rare but has been documented. Males create a small spawning depression or select a flat surface (broad plant leaves, smooth rock) where the female deposits eggs; the male then guards and fans the clutch until hatching, typically within 5–7 days. Fry are minute and require live microscopic foods (infusoria, rotifers) for early growth, making successful rearing highly challenging. No established captive-breeding protocol exists for hobbyists. Brackish conditions and a heavily planted, species-only setup are considered prerequisites for any breeding attempt.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 10 Pufferfish You Can Keep in Freshwater and Brackish Aquariums
primary.notes: Primary trade/hobbyist source. Confirmed brackish requirement (SG 1.005–1.008), adult size 5–8 cm, temperature 22–26°C (72–79°F), alkaline pH, feeding on pest snails and frozen foods, and compatibility notes (bumblebee gobies, mollies). Tank size guidance: 20-gallon long minimum.

additional.name: FishBase — Dichotomyctere ocellatus (Eyespot Pufferfish)
additional.notes: Confirmed current accepted name Dichotomyctere ocellatus (Steindachner, 1870); max size 8.0 cm TL; temperature 22–26°C; native distribution Asia (Indochina, Malaysia, Indonesia); taxonomy Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes; diet: snails and benthic organisms. FishBase lists as freshwater demersal but notes commercial aquarium trade use. Trophic level 3.4.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — In Search of the Peaceful Puffer
additional.notes: Described as 6 cm SE Asian species; ideal SG 1.005; greenish-brown with yellow figure-8 markings; juveniles more peaceful than adults; adults territorial fin-nippers; bumblebee gobies noted as safer tankmates. Treated as dwarf cichlid equivalent in temperament. Confirmed as Tetraodon biocellatus (synonym).

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Figure of Eight Puffer
additional.notes: Provided pH 7.2–8.2, hardness 12–25 dH, temperature 22–26°C, adult size 8 cm; native range Cambodia, Indonesia, Malaysia; diet: frozen/live mysis shrimp, mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, krill, cockle and mussel; breeding noted as extremely rare, eggs scattered among plants with male guarding; species-only tank recommended.

additional.name: Guidarium — Figure 8 Puffer (Brackish) Care Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed SG 1.005–1.008, pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 10–25 dGH, temperature 24–28°C, minimum 60 L / 60 cm tank; aggression level high (8/10); fin-nipper, snail eater, shrimp eater, territorial; best kept singly or species-only; sand substrate for foraging behaviour recommended.

fish.breedingNotes: Extremely rare in captivity. Male guards eggs on flat surface; fry require live micro-foods (rotifers, infusoria). No established hobbyist breeding protocol.
